papers.title: The role of stellar collisions for the formation of massive stars

abstract.text: We use direct N-body simulations of gas embedded star clusters to study the importance of stellar collisions for the formation and mass accretion history of high-mass stars. Our clusters start in virial equilibrium as a mix of gas and proto-stars. Proto-stars then accrete matter using different mass accretion rates and the amount of gas is reduced in the same way as the mass of stars increases. During the simulations we check for stellar collisions and we investigate the role of these collisions for the build-up of high-mass stars and the formation of runaway stars. We find that a significant number of collisions only occur in clusters with initial half-mass radii r_h < 0.1 pc. After emerging from their parental gas clouds, such clusters end up too compact compared to observed young, massive open clusters. In addition, collisions lead mainly to the formation of a single runaway star instead of the formation of many high mass stars with a broad mass spectrum. We therefore conclude that massive stars form mainly by gas accretion, with stellar collisions only playing a minor role if any at all. Collisions of stars in the pre-main sequence phase might however contribute to the formation of the most massive stars in the densest star clusters and possibly to the formation of intermediate-mass black holes with masses up to a few 100 Msun.

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Models: The paper proposes a GPU-based $N$-body simulation method that models star cluster evolution by incorporating gas dynamics through a modified stellar equation of motion, with star formation, accretion, and gas removal governed by physical prescriptions consistent with observed star formation efficiencies and initial mass functions. Results: Simulations show that mass-segregated clusters with initial half-mass radii of 0.033–1.0 pc evolve to form compact cores hosting massive stars, with final cluster radii remaining nearly constant across initial conditions in compact clusters and core collapse occurring only after gas expulsion in extended clusters, highlighting the critical role of mass segregation in the formation of O stars. Conclusions: The simulations conclude that high-mass stars do not form through collisions between lower-mass stars, as such collisions are rare in observed cluster conditions and would instead produce extremely massive single runaways rather than the observed initial mass function; accretion via discs is the more plausible mechanism for massive star formation. Introduction: This paper presents the first direct $N$-body simulations of proto-cluster evolution, demonstrating how stellar collisions in dense, accreting environments enable the formation of massive stars by overcoming radiation pressure limits through cluster dynamics and gas expulsion.

title: The role of stellar collisions for the formation of massive stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical N-body simulation study of stellar collision mechanisms for runaway star formation, no observational data presented
